claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You do not live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 1 child:
  - Alex is 13 years old and lives with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant does not live in the UK
  - Child is 13 (under 16)
  - Another claimant who lives with the child has priority
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Claimant does not live in the UK; Another claimant who lives with the child has priority
circumstances: Claimant does not live in the UK
Another claimant who lives with the child has priority
Child is not in local authority care
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: I'll help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit. Let me start the assessment process.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great! To start, I need to know a bit about your child (or children). 

**First, how old is your child?** This will help me determine which eligibility path to follow:

- **Under 16** - Generally easier to qualify
- **16 to 19** - May still qualify if in approved education or training
- **20 or over** - Generally not eligible

If you have multiple children, let's start with one child at a time. Please tell me:
1. Your child's name (or just "Child 1" if you prefer)
2. Their age

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Hi, thanks for helping me with this. I have one child - Alex, who's 13 years old. I'd like to find out if I can claim Child Benefit for them.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Perfect! Thank you for that information. Let me work through this with you.

You have **Alex, who is 13 years old**. Since Alex is under 16, that's a good start for eligibility.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Now, the next question:

**Is Alex working less than 24 hours per week (or not in paid work at all)?**

This is important because if a child is working 24 or more hours per week, they may not qualify for Child Benefit. For most 13-year-olds, this isn't usually an issue, but I need to confirm.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, Alex doesn't work at all. They're only 13, so they're just in school.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Excellent, that's what I expected for a 13-year-old. Alex is in school and not working, which is good for eligibility.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Good! Next question:

**Is Alex receiving any benefits in their own right?** For example:
- Employment and Support Allowance (ESA)
- Universal Credit
- Other benefits claimed in their own name

For most 13-year-olds in school, this typically doesn't apply, but I need to check to be thorough.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, Alex doesn't get any benefits in their own name. They're just a regular school kid.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Perfect! That's completely normal for a 13-year-old in school.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Next question:

**Is Alex in an apprenticeship program?**

Again, for a 13-year-old in regular school, this typically wouldn't apply, but I need to check. Certain types of apprenticeships can affect Child Benefit eligibility depending on which part of the UK you're in.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, Alex isn't doing an apprenticeship. They're just in regular school.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Great! Alex is in regular school, not an apprenticeship.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Now we need to check your residency status. This is an important requirement for Child Benefit.

**Do you meet the UK residency requirements?** You qualify if ANY of the following applies to you:

1. **You normally live in the UK** (most common)
2. You have settled status under the EU Settlement Scheme
3. You have pre-settled status and are working (earning above the National Insurance threshold)
4. You have pre-settled status and are actively seeking work (up to 91 days)
5. You have pre-settled status with sufficient resources to support yourself
6. You have pre-settled status as a student with sufficient resources
7. You have pre-settled status as a family member of an EEA/Swiss national with right to reside
8. You're living abroad as a Crown servant or in certain qualifying countries

**Do any of these apply to you?** If yes, which one?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Actually, I don't live in the UK. I live abroad at the moment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
I see. Living abroad doesn't automatically disqualify you, but there are specific circumstances where you can still claim.

**Can you tell me more about your situation?** Specifically:

1. **Are you a Crown servant** (working for the UK government abroad, such as in the diplomatic service or armed forces)?

2. **Are you living in certain qualifying countries** that have agreements with the UK?

3. **Or are you simply living abroad for other reasons** (work, family, etc.)?

This will help me determine if you can still qualify for Child Benefit despite living outside the UK.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: I'm just living abroad for other reasons - nothing to do with government work or anything like that.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Thank you for clarifying. Unfortunately, this affects your eligibility for Child Benefit.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
## Assessment Result: Not Eligible ❌

I'm sorry, but **you are not eligible for Child Benefit** for Alex at this time.

### Why?

While **Alex qualifies as an eligible child** (being 13 years old, in school, not working, and not receiving benefits), **you do not meet the UK residency requirements** for claiming Child Benefit.

### The Residency Requirement

To claim Child Benefit, you must either:
- **Normally live in the UK**, OR
- Meet special circumstances such as:
  - Having settled or pre-settled status under the EU Settlement Scheme (with qualifying conditions)
  - Working abroad as a Crown servant (UK government employee)
  - Living in certain countries with reciprocal agreements with the UK

Since you're living abroad for personal reasons without any of these qualifying exceptions, you cannot claim Child Benefit.

### What You Can Do

- **If you return to live in the UK**, you would be able to claim Child Benefit for Alex at that time
- **If your circumstances change** (for example, if you become a Crown servant), you may become eligible
- Keep documentation of Alex's details for when you may be eligible in the future

If you have any questions about this decision or think your situation might change, please let me know. I'm here to help clarify anything!

reasonings: Alex is 13 years old (under 16), not working, not receiving benefits in their own right, and not in an apprenticeship - all of which would normally support eligibility. However, the claimant does not meet the UK residency requirements. The claimant lives abroad for personal reasons and is not a Crown servant, does not have settled/pre-settled status under the EU Settlement Scheme, and is not living in a qualifying country with a reciprocal agreement. UK Child Benefit requires that claimants normally live in the UK or meet one of the special circumstance exceptions. Since none of these apply, the claim for Alex is not eligible.
arguments.overall_reasoning: The claimant has one child, Alex (age 13), who would otherwise qualify as a qualifying child. However, the claimant fails to meet the fundamental UK residency requirement for Child Benefit, as they live abroad for personal reasons without any of the qualifying exceptions (Crown servant status, EU Settlement Scheme status, or residence in a qualifying country). Therefore, they are not eligible for Child Benefit.

response.description: Determine child's age category for eligibility
response.routing_logic: Under 16: automatic qualification. 16-19: check education/training. 20+: not eligible

response.question: Is the child working less than 24 hours per week (or not in paid work)?
response.help_text: Children aged 16+ working 24+ hours per week and not in approved education/training are not eligible

response.question: Is the child NOT receiving certain benefits in their own right (e.g., ESA, Universal Credit)?
response.help_text: Children receiving Employment and Support Allowance or Universal Credit in their own right are not eligible

response.question: Is the child NOT in a disqualifying apprenticeship?
response.help_text: Regional rules apply: English apprenticeships (all types) disqualify. Scottish Modern Apprenticeships disqualify. Welsh Foundation Apprenticeships are APPROVED. Other Welsh apprenticeships disqualify.

response.question: Does the claimant meet UK residency requirements?
response.help_text: Must normally live in the UK or meet special circumstances

paths.requirement: Claimant normally lives in the UK

paths.description: Has settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
paths.requirement: Has settled status under EU Settlement Scheme

criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Working and earning/expect to earn above primary National Insurance threshold for 3 continuous months

paths.description: Pre-settled status and actively seeking work
criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Looking for work (can claim as jobseeker for up to 91 days)
Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Has sufficient resources to support self and family financially
NOT claiming Income Support, income-based JSA, Pension Credit, or Universal Credit
Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Studying and has sufficient resources to support self financially

paths.description: Pre-settled status as family member of EEA/Swiss national
criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Is family member of EEA or Swiss national with right to reside
One of: spouse/civil partner, dependent child/grandchild under 21, dependent parent/grandparent

paths.requirement: Living abroad as Crown servant or in certain qualifying countries

response.evaluation_logic: If ANY path is satisfied, proceed to responsibility check

response.reason: Does not meet UK residency requirements
response.guidance: Must normally live in the UK or meet special circumstances (settled status, pre-settled status with qualifying criteria, Crown servant, or living in certain countries). If you have pre-settled status, you may need to be working, studying, or have sufficient resources.

UK Child Benefit requires that claimants normally live in the UK or meet one of the special circumstance exceptions. Since none of these apply, the claim for Alex is not eligible.

However, the claimant fails to meet the fundamental UK residency requirement for Child Benefit, as they live abroad for personal reasons without any of the qualifying exceptions (Crown servant status, EU Settlement Scheme status, or residence in a qualifying country).
